primary_diagnosis.name: Erythema multiforme minor, secondary to recent herpes simplex virus (cold sore) infection, with classic acral target/iris lesions and centripetal spread and no mucosal involvement

differential.evidence_basis: Sudden symmetrical eruption of classic three-zone target/iris lesions on the acral extensor surfaces (hands/wrists) with centripetal spread, following a herpes labialis outbreak ~10 days earlier — the defining picture of HSV-triggered erythema multiforme minor.

differential.diagnosis: Stevens-Johnson Syndrome / toxic epidermal necrolysis (severe mucocutaneous reaction / EM major end of spectrum)
differential.evidence_basis: Any target-lesion or blistering eruption mandates screening for the catastrophic end of the spectrum — mucosal (mouth/eye/genital) erosions, skin pain, Nikolsky-positive sloughing, high fever and a drug trigger — none of which can be fully examined for over telehealth.

Also acute and itchy, but urticarial wheals are transient/migratory and blanching without a fixed three-zone target morphology or central epidermal (dusky/blistered) change; the fixed target lesions here argue against it.

Can produce dusky targetoid plaques, but is typically solitary or few, localised and recurs at the same site on drug re-exposure; the generalised symmetrical acral distribution and clear HSV (not drug) trigger argue against it.
A mimic especially in the anxious patient, but lesions are evanescent (individual lesions resolve within 24 hours) and lack the fixed dusky/blistered centre of true target lesions.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening the leading diagnosis is HSV-associated erythema multiforme minor; the important-not-to-miss consideration is a severe mucocutaneous reaction (SJS/TEN), which drives the telehealth red-flag screen and the safety-net rather than the baseline tier.

differential.evidence_basis: No mucosal erosions, no skin sloughing/peeling, no severe skin pain, no high fever, no new drug and no airway/anaphylaxis features — a benign self-limiting erythema multiforme minor, not a systemic or severe mucocutaneous disorder.

Screened negative now (no mucosal involvement, no skin pain/detachment, no drug trigger, systemically well) but remains the catastrophic mimic to keep a prominent safety-net for; the mucosae and Nikolsky sign cannot be fully examined remotely.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The red-flag screen removes SJS/TEN and anaphylaxis and confirms a telehealth/GP-manageable erythema multiforme minor. The rapid spread and distress justify reassurance and review to lay eyes on the mucosae, but NOT urgent same-day ED or 000.

differential.diagnosis: Erythema multiforme minor, HSV-triggered, without mucosal involvement
differential.evidence_basis: Classic acral target lesions, a preceding cold sore, no mucosal/systemic involvement and a negative SJS/TEN and anaphylaxis screen; the correct endpoint is reassurance, symptomatic care (mild topical corticosteroid + simple analgesia + cool compresses) and review, rather than emergency escalation.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The correct end-point is recognition of a benign, self-limiting HSV-associated erythema multiforme minor, strong reassurance, patient-performable symptomatic measures, and review to confirm the minor variant and inspect the mucosae — with explicit safety-netting for progression to SJS/TEN and for anaphylaxis. Reflexive escalation to 000/ED is over-triage.

red_flags.description: Severe mucocutaneous reaction (Stevens-Johnson Syndrome / toxic epidermal necrolysis, or progression to EM major) — painful mucosal (mouth/eye/genital) erosions or blistering, skin peeling/sloughing in sheets, positive Nikolsky sign, high fever or systemic upset.
red_flags.clinical_significance: The dermatological emergency at the severe end of the erythema-multiforme spectrum; SJS/TEN carries high mortality and needs urgent hospital assessment, and the mucosae and Nikolsky sign cannot be fully examined over telehealth.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed or progressing SJS/TEN leads to extensive epidermal and mucosal loss with a high risk of death; delay is catastrophic.

red_flags.description: Evolving systemic allergic reaction / anaphylaxis or airway angioedema — swelling of the face, lips, tongue or throat, difficulty breathing, widespread urticaria or faintness.
red_flags.clinical_significance: The immediately life-threatening pathway the distressed patient and the telephone triager feared ('severe allergic reaction'); airway angioedema/anaphylaxis needs adrenaline and an ambulance. Breath-Ezy cannot give IM adrenaline, so recognition and immediate 000 escalation is the correct action.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Unrecognised airway angioedema/anaphylaxis can cause airway obstruction and death.

red_flags.description: New ocular symptoms — eye pain, redness, gritty sensation, discharge or visual disturbance — suggesting ocular mucosal involvement.
red_flags.clinical_significance: Ocular involvement is a marker of transition toward the major (SJS) end of the spectrum and threatens sight; it needs urgent ophthalmology and cannot be examined over telehealth.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Untreated ocular mucosal involvement can cause scarring and permanent visual loss.

pathophysiology_summary: Erythema multiforme is an acute, immune-mediated hypersensitivity reaction of the skin, most commonly triggered by infection — herpes simplex virus (HSV) is the single most frequent precipitant, typically 1-2 weeks after a cold-sore outbreak. HSV DNA fragments are transported to keratinocytes, and an HSV-specific CD4+ T-cell response drives interferon-gamma-mediated cytotoxic injury at the dermo-epidermal junction, producing the characteristic lesion: a fixed, symmetrical, acrally-distributed target/iris lesion with three concentric zones (a dusky, sometimes vesiculated or crusted epidermal-damage centre, a paler oedematous middle ring, and an erythematous outer halo). Erythema multiforme MINOR involves little or no mucosa and no significant systemic upset and is self-limiting, resolving over 1-3 weeks, often with transient post-inflammatory pigmentation. It sits at the benign end of a spectrum whose severe pole — erythema multiforme major and the distinct entities Stevens-Johnson Syndrome and toxic epidermal necrolysis (more often drug-triggered) — is defined by extensive painful mucosal erosions, epidermal detachment (positive Nikolsky sign) and systemic toxicity. The whole clinical task is therefore to recognise the benign minor variant from the classic morphology and HSV trigger while actively excluding the catastrophic severe-spectrum mimic; because the mucosae, skin integrity and Nikolsky sign cannot be fully examined over telehealth, that exclusion is a directed history screen plus review, not a remote certainty.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Being swept along by the sudden onset, the frightening 'bullseye' appearance, the rapid centripetal spread and the triage note's SJS/TEN and anaphylaxis framing into calling 000 / sending to ED, when the red-flag screen for mucosal involvement, skin sloughing, high fever and airway symptoms is negative and erythema multiforme minor is telehealth/GP-manageable.

Treating any target lesion or central blister as automatically SJS/TEN rather than recognising that erythema multiforme minor is the common, benign cause of target lesions; the acral distribution, HSV trigger, absent mucosal involvement and mild symptoms point to the minor variant.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Missing the SJS/TEN and anaphylaxis screen
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Correctly labelling erythema multiforme but never asking about mucosal (mouth/eye/genital) erosions, skin pain/sloughing, high fever, a new drug, or facial/airway swelling — omitting the one screen that separates benign EM minor from the lethal mimic that telehealth cannot examine for.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Reaching for systemic corticosteroids or antivirals in a single benign episode
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Erythema multiforme minor is self-limiting; systemic corticosteroids are not indicated (and are of uncertain benefit) for a single mild episode, and an antiviral is not needed acutely for the already-resolving cold sore. Antiviral SUPPRESSION is a consideration only for frequently RECURRENT HSV-associated EM, decided at review — not this acute encounter.

Recommending topical antihistamine or topical local-anaesthetic 'itch' creams, which are themselves common contact sensitisers and can complicate an inflammatory dermatosis — a mild topical corticosteroid and simple oral analgesia are the appropriate symptomatic choices.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Direct examination of the oral, conjunctival and genital mucosae to definitively exclude the mucosal erosions of SJS/TEN (source in-person exam found NO mucosal erosions or blistering)
Elicitation of Nikolsky sign and assessment for epidermal detachment/sloughing (source in-person exam found NO sloughing and a negative Nikolsky sign)
Close inspection of individual target lesions to confirm the three-zone morphology and central epidermal change
Exact blood pressure, heart rate, temperature and respiratory rate without a home device (source in-person values BP 125/80, HR 88, Temp 37.4, RR 16, SpO2 99%, Wt 76 kg)
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise HSV-associated erythema multiforme minor from the classic target-lesion morphology, acral distribution and preceding cold sore; provide strong reassurance that this is a benign, self-limiting immune reaction to the cold-sore virus and not a live skin infection or a dangerous allergy; instruct patient-performable symptomatic care (cool damp compresses instead of scratching, do not pop the central blisters, a mild topical corticosteroid and simple analgesia for comfort); and arrange review (routine GP review within 7-10 days, or promptly/sooner if the patient wants earlier reassurance) to confirm the minor variant and lay eyes on the mucosae. Escalation to 000/ED is reserved for the red-flag safety-net triggers (mucosal involvement / skin peeling / high fever -> SJS-TEN; facial/airway swelling or breathing difficulty -> anaphylaxis), not the baseline.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low for the diagnosis (the target morphology, acral distribution and HSV trigger are classic). The dominant risks are OVER-action (reflexively escalating a distressing but benign target-lesion eruption to ED/000) and UNDER-screening the severe-spectrum mimic (SJS/TEN mucosal/skin involvement) that telehealth cannot examine for.
